title: CEO Interview with Tom (TJ) Jackson of Softchip - Semiwiki
gist: Softchip CEO Tom Jackson describes DRDCL (Dynamically Reconfigurable Differential Cascode Logic), a new circuit-level logic structure built on IBM-heritage differential cascode logic that claims 134x-5,200x effective-density gains over fixed CMOS, targeting AI inference bottlenecks like KV-cache movement via its 'Soft-NMC' near-memory compute product, licensed as IP rather than sold as chips.

themes.theme: DRDCL: a circuit-level rather than architectural bet

insights.claim: DRDCL (Dynamically Reconfigurable Differential Cascode Logic) uses a shared differential cascode evaluation tree plus a patented Cross-Switch circuit to produce multiple useful outputs from a single evaluation, reconfiguring which function is expressed at nanosecond scale without LUTs, bitstreams, or FPGA-style routing fabric.
insights.speaker: Tom Jackson
insights.so_what: Distinguishes Softchip from FPGA-style reconfigurable logic vendors — claims to reconfigure at the circuit level rather than through a separate configuration layer, which if validated could be a genuinely novel logic-density lever for AI hardware.
insights.quote: DRDCL does not use lookup tables, multiplexer fabrics, or configuration bitstreams. It uses the intrinsic evaluation structure of differential cascode trees with programmable output routing, so reconfiguration happens at the circuit level in nanoseconds rather than through a separate configuration layer.

insights.claim: The underlying differential cascode logic foundation (DCVL/DCVS) is already inherently at least 2x faster and at least 2x lower dynamic power than conventional CMOS logic, before DRDCL's added multi-output and reconfiguration layers.
insights.speaker: Tom Jackson
insights.so_what: Baseline 2x speed/power claim versus CMOS is the foundation of Softchip's entire pitch — a benchmark investors should watch for independent silicon validation against.
insights.quote: The differential cascode foundation is already powerful. DCVL/DCVS is inherently at least 2x speed and at least 2x lower dynamic power versus conventional CMOS logic.

insights.claim: Softchip claims effective-density gains versus fixed CMOS of 134x for a 3-high DRDCL tree, 1,200x for 4-high, and 5,200x for 5-high trees, with density gains growing exponentially as tree height increases.
insights.speaker: Tom Jackson
insights.so_what: Extremely aggressive claimed scaling (134x to 5,200x) is the headline number to stress-test against first-silicon characterization; if even partially validated it would be a step-function improvement in logic density.
insights.quote: Effective-density gain vs. fixed CMOS: 134x for 3-high, 1,200x for 4-high, and 5,200x for 5-high DRDCL trees.

insights.claim: CTO Jim Davis previously led IBM's VLSI Silicon effort and is co-inventor of the foundational differential cascode logic that DRDCL builds upon.
insights.speaker: Tom Jackson
insights.so_what: Credentialed IBM/Intel pedigree lends technical credibility to an otherwise unproven circuit-level claim — useful for assessing founder/team risk in early-stage semiconductor IP diligence.
insights.quote: CTO Jim Davis previously led IBM’s VLSI Silicon effort and is co- inventor of the foundational differential cascode logic.

themes.theme: Soft-NMC: targeting the AI inference KV-cache bottleneck

insights.claim: Softchip's first application, Soft-NMC (near-memory compute for AI inference), applies DRDCL-based logic near the memory interface to perform attention reduction and control work closer to where KV-cache data resides, targeting a 60-80% reduction in repeated KV/attention movement in selected decode paths.
insights.speaker: Tom Jackson
insights.so_what: Targets a well-known, quantified AI inference bottleneck (transformer decode KV-cache movement) — a claim worth tracking against incumbent near-memory-compute and compute-in-memory approaches from larger players.
insights.quote: At the traffic level, our architecture model targets 60-80% reduction in repeated KV/attention movement in selected decode paths.

insights.claim: Softchip's system-level model estimates roughly 12-13x local improvement at the memory-interface path and 3-4x sustained tokens/sec improvement in decode-dominated, KV-bound inference scenarios.
insights.speaker: Tom Jackson
insights.so_what: A concrete, model-based (not yet silicon-validated) tokens/sec improvement claim (3-4x) that would be highly material to AI inference economics if it holds up in real hardware.
insights.quote: At the system level, our model estimates approximately 12-13x local improvement at the memory-interface path and 3-4x sustained tokens/sec improvement in decode-dominated, KV-bound scenarios.

insights.claim: For its first logic-block family, Softchip targets a conservative 2-5x useful throughput per area/power versus equivalent CMOS as a first-silicon target, 5-10x as a stronger product target, and 10x+ upside pending silicon characterization.
insights.speaker: Tom Jackson
insights.so_what: Gives investors a tiered, self-disclosed confidence scale (conservative to aspirational) for evaluating Softchip's PPA claims as silicon data becomes available.
insights.quote: our target is multi-x PPA improvement versus equivalent CMOS in selected paths: 2-5x useful throughput per area/power as a conservative first-silicon target, 5-10x effective PPA as a stronger product target, and 10x+ upside

themes.theme: Business model: IP licensing, not chip manufacturing

insights.claim: Softchip positions itself as an IP licensing company, not a chip company, aiming to work with a small number of semiconductor, foundry, memory-interface, and AI hardware partners via NRE-funded logic-block evaluation leading to IP licensing.
insights.speaker: Tom Jackson
insights.so_what: Fabless IP-licensing model (similar to Arm/CAST-style businesses) reduces capital intensity but makes revenue dependent on partner adoption cycles and successful silicon characterization against CMOS baselines.
insights.quote: SoftChip is an IP company, not a chip company. We aim to work with a small number of semiconductor, foundry, memory-interface, and AI hardware partners to validate DRDCL IP against CMOS baselines on real process paths.

insights.claim: Softchip is developing two additional DRDCL-based products beyond Soft-NMC: ARMOR, a resilience/remapping layer targeting under 1% area overhead to improve effective yield in accelerator tiles, and Soft-TTC for timing/throughput/control (scheduling, arbitration, routing), targeting 2-5x effective PPA improvement in those paths.
insights.speaker: Tom Jackson
insights.so_what: Shows a roadmap of DRDCL applications beyond inference memory bottlenecks (yield/resilience and control-plane logic), broadening the addressable use cases if the core circuit claim proves out.
insights.quote: The first is ARMOR, a resilience and remapping concept. The thesis is that DRDCL can cover broad regions of digital logic with a very small reconfigurable repair layer, targeting under 1% area overhead.
